questions.question: What is the primary purpose of a buffer pool in a database storage engine?
options: To compress pages before writing them to disk
To cache recently accessed disk pages in memory to avoid slow I/O
To reorder write operations for better sequential throughput
To encrypt pages at rest
questions.explanation: A buffer pool sits between the storage engine and the disk manager, keeping frequently accessed pages in memory and dramatically reducing the number of slow disk reads.

questions.question: What does it mean to 'pin' a page in a buffer pool?
options: Encrypt the page so it cannot be read by other threads
Mark the page so the replacement policy prefers it for eviction
Increment a reference count that prevents the page from being evicted
Write the page to disk immediately
questions.explanation: Pinning increments the page's pin count. A page with pin_count > 0 is in use and cannot be evicted. Unpin releases the reference when the storage engine is done.

questions.question: How does the Clock (second-chance) replacement algorithm work?
options: It evicts the page that was least frequently used by tracking hit counters
It maintains a sorted list and evicts from the tail every clock tick
It sweeps a circular hand; pages with ref_bit=1 get their bit cleared and survive, pages with ref_bit=0 are evicted
It randomly selects a page to evict to avoid worst-case patterns
questions.explanation: The Clock algorithm sweeps a circular hand through frames. If the ref_bit is 1, it clears it (second chance) and moves on. If ref_bit is 0, that page is evicted. Pinned pages are always skipped.

questions.question: What is the 'double buffering problem' in database systems?
options: Using two separate buffer pools for indexes and heap data
The same page being cached in both the OS page cache and the database buffer pool, wasting memory
Mirroring the buffer pool across two servers for high availability
Holding two versions of the same page for MVCC visibility
questions.explanation: When the database uses buffered I/O, the OS also caches file blocks in its page cache. This means the same data exists in two caches, wasting memory. Solutions include O_DIRECT (bypassing the OS cache) and posix_fadvise.

questions.question: When is a dirty page written back to disk?
options: Immediately after every modification
Only when the page is evicted from the buffer pool, or when explicitly flushed (e.g., by a background writer or checkpointer)
Never — dirty pages are only written during database shutdown
Every time the pin count reaches zero
questions.explanation: Dirty pages are written back on eviction (to make room) or during explicit flush operations (background writer, checkpointer, or manual flush). Writing immediately would defeat the purpose of caching.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a page replacement policy used in databases?
options: LRU (Least Recently Used)
Clock (Second-Chance)
ARC (Adaptive Replacement Cache)
ECS (Earliest Created Segment)
questions.explanation: LRU, Clock, and ARC are all real replacement policies. 'ECS' is not a known page replacement algorithm. ARC is used by ZFS and InnoDB; Clock variants are used by PostgreSQL; LRU with midpoint insertion is used by InnoDB.
